network.overall_strategy: The network follows the literature consensus that prebiotic chemistry robustly generates racemic alanine through multiple convergent routes centered on HCN, acetaldehyde, α-aminopropionitrile, and pyruvate, while L-alanine is most plausibly obtained only after a later terrestrial symmetry-breaking/amplification stage rather than by any single direct abiotic synthesis. Accordingly, the network explicitly models ten literature-grounded pathways that produce DL-alanine or its immediate precursors across hydrothermal and surface settings, then connects these products into a biochemical/prebiotic selection module in which L-alanine emerges as a target through network-level chiral amplification associated with magnetite/CISS-informed and peptide-selection scenarios. This preserves fidelity to the research findings while satisfying the requested target specification.

reactions.name: Classic Miller-Urey spark generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: TiO2-assisted surface concentration following spark discharge
conditions.other: electrical discharge in reducing gas mixture for ~1 week; mineral shore deposition captures soluble HCN and acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates nitriles and aldehydes in the atmosphere; products dissolve into surface waters and are concentrated on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Miller's 1953 apparatus and Miller's 1955 mechanistic analysis showed that spark discharge from CH4/NH3/H2/H2O generates the precursors that enter Strecker chemistry, including HCN and acetaldehyde, from which alanine forms in the aqueous phase. TiO2 and montmorillonite are included here as surface mineral agents for photochemical exposure and concentration in the required surface-environment representation, but the core precursor generation follows the discharge literature rather than mineral catalysis per se (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Volcanic H2S spark generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide mineral capture under volcanic surface conditions
conditions.other: spark discharge in CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S system; volcanic mist/wet deposition; H2S-rich variant

reactions.mechanism: Volcanic spark chemistry produces richer nitrile/aldehyde inventories than the classic experiment; products are trapped in surface condensates.
reactions.reasoning: Archived H2S-containing Miller experiments yielded alanine-enhanced amino acid inventories and operate through the same precursor logic of in situ nitrile and carbonyl production. Surface sulfide minerals are consistent with volcanic settings emphasized in the prompt and with sulfur-rich discharge conditions (Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Fe(Ni)S-like sulfide surfaces represented by greigite and mackinawite
conditions.other: alkaline vent-like setting, pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinization conditions

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reduction of CO2 by H2 on iron-sulfur surfaces generates reduced C1 compounds including formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Herschy et al. demonstrated vent-like production of formaldehyde from H2 and CO2 on Fe(Ni)S catalysts under alkaline hydrothermal conditions, supporting hydrothermal supply of reduced carbon to downstream surface chemistry. Greigite and mackinawite are used as literature-consistent Fe-S agents within the allowed hydrothermal catalyst set (Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: Fe/Ni-like mineral surfaces approximated by magnetite and greigite
conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal or vent-sediment interface; H2-fed carbon fixation; rTCA-like and nanoparticle-enabled chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed CO2 reduction and C-C coupling produce keto-acids, including pyruvate, in hydrothermal networks.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a key literature-supported branch point for alanine synthesis. Beyazay et al. reported pyruvate formation from CO2 reduction over Fe/Ni nanoparticles, while Muchowska et al. showed iron-promoted rTCA-like chemistry generating central metabolic intermediates. This reaction is intentionally represented as a hydrothermal pyruvate-supply node rather than a one-step fully resolved mechanism because the network must connect known pyruvate-dependent alanine pathways without contradicting the literature (Muchowska et al., 2019; Beyazay et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical aldehyde homologation to acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox on TiO2 with sulfide-mineral-associated cyanosulfidic conditions
conditions.other: UV irradiation; neutral to mildly basic aqueous conditions; H2S-assisted reductive homologation

reactions.mechanism: HCN-derived photoredox homologation and reduction of carbonyl intermediates furnishes aldehydes including acetaldehyde in cyanosulfidic networks.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland established photochemical routes to simple sugar and aldehyde precursors from HCN-derived chemistry, and Patel et al. integrated HCN/H2S/UV/phosphate systems into cyanosulfidic networks that feed amino acid precursor synthesis. This node is kept at the acetaldehyde level because acetaldehyde is the directly documented Strecker bottleneck for alanine (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker addition from acetaldehyde to alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions; acidic or basic pH possible; imine formation followed by cyanide addition

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, then cyanide adds to form the aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The classical Strecker route for alanine proceeds via acetaldehyde imine and α-aminopropionitrile under mild aqueous conditions. Miller identified Strecker chemistry as the operative mechanism in spark-discharge amino acid synthesis, and later isotope work confirmed α-APN as the diagnostic precursor. Mineral inclusion here reflects surface concentration rather than a claim of mandatory catalytic participation in the original Strecker studies (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker synthesis of alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: phosphate-associated cyanosulfidic medium with sulfide mineral participation
conditions.other: UV-enabled Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cyanosulfidic setting; neutral pH; reported ~70% α-aminopropionitrile from acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Phosphoro-Strecker/cyanosulfidic chemistry channels acetaldehyde into α-aminopropionitrile under HCN/H2S/UV conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. reported systems chemistry in which HCN, H2S, UV light, and phosphate support production of amino acid precursors, with α-aminopropionitrile obtained in high yield from acetaldehyde under phosphoro-Strecker conditions. This is one of the most direct literature-supported surface routes to the alanine precursor (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile to alaninamide

conditions.other: sequential nitrile hydration under mild surface-pool conditions

reactions.mechanism: Partial hydrolysis of the nitrile gives the corresponding amide intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker pathway to alanine proceeds through stepwise conversion of α-aminopropionitrile to alaninamide and then alanine. This representation resolves the previously over-compressed nitrile-to-amino-acid step and follows the classical mechanistic sequence emphasized in the literature (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alaninamide to DL-alanine

conditions.other: continued hydrolysis after aminonitrile hydration

reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis furnishes racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Sequential hydrolysis of the Strecker aminonitrile through alaninamide to alanine is the standard prebiotic route documented since Strecker and interpreted mechanistically for Miller-type syntheses. The product is racemic, consistent with the literature review (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Pyrite-coupled reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: FeS/H2S to FeS2 redox coupling on iron-sulfur minerals
conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal conditions; oxidative pyrite formation supplies reducing equivalents

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes reductive amination with ammonia, driven by FeS/H2S to pyrite redox chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Hafenbradl et al. first demonstrated prebiotic alanine synthesis from pyruvate and ammonia coupled to oxidative pyrite formation. This is one of the most direct and historically important hydrothermal routes to racemic alanine, and explicitly grounds alanine synthesis in iron-sulfur vent chemistry (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Wächtershäuser, 1988).

reactions.name: Mixed-valence iron mineral reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.other: alkaline conditions; maximum yield near 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III); vent-sediment compatible

reactions.mechanism: Iron-mediated electron transfer drives reductive amination of pyruvate by ammonia to alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. showed that mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides promote pyruvate-to-alanine conversion under alkaline conditions, with best performance at Fe(II):Fe(III) ~1:1. Magnetite plus Fe0 is used here as an allowed hydrothermal mineral representation of reduced/mixed-valence iron chemistry in vent-adjacent sediments (Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Ni-H2-like catalytic reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine under mild prebiotic conditions

conditions.other: pH 7-8; 30 mM pyruvate; mild aqueous interfacial setting

reactions.mechanism: Ni-catalyzed H2-driven reductive amination converts pyruvate and ammonium/ammonia to alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. showed that nickel catalysts enable H2 gas at 5 bar to reduce pyruvate with ammonia to alanine at 22°C and pH 7-8, closely paralleling enzymatic biosynthesis. Because the prompt only mandates minerals for hydrothermal and surface reactions, this recent mild catalytic system is placed in the biochemical/prebiotic stage to preserve exact literature conditions (Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs capture of pyruvate-derived aminonitrile to 5-methylhydantoin

conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs-type chemistry; cyanide and ammonia addition; CO2 capture into hydantoin

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate forms an aminonitrile adduct with ammonia/cyanide, then CO2 capture cyclizes the intermediate to 5-methylhydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. highlighted that pyruvate reacts with cyanide and ammonia and captures CO2 released in the sequence to form 5-methylhydantoin, linking amino acid and pyrimidine precursor chemistry. This reaction is represented as a distinct hydantoin-forming node to avoid previous over-compression (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine

conditions.other: 80°C hydrolysis step in Bucherer-Bergs sequence

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening of hydantoin under hydrolytic conditions gives N-carbamoyl alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs route proceeds through hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine prior to alanine release. Keeping this step discrete follows the recent literature and fixes prior under-resolution of the hydantoin path (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to DL-alanine

conditions.other: continued hydrolysis in Bucherer-Bergs sequence

reactions.mechanism: Decarbamoylation/hydrolysis releases racemic alanine from N-carbamoyl alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to alanine completes the hydantoin route described by Pulletikurti et al. The route is experimentally important because it offers a non-Strecker path from pyruvate to alanine while connecting to nucleobase precursor chemistry (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN in concentrated eutectic or evaporitic settings

conditions.other: requires concentrated HCN, typically >0.01 M; eutectic freezing or evaporative concentration

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes through dimeric and trimeric nitrile intermediates to tetrameric DAMN.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization to DAMN is a classic and highly connected branch in prebiotic chemistry. The literature emphasizes concentrated HCN, especially in eutectic environments, as the gateway to both nucleobase and amino-acid-yielding polymer chemistry. DAMN is represented as a gateway node rather than being directly collapsed to alanine (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer material to DL-alanine-containing amino acid mixture

conditions.other: alkaline aging followed by hydrolysis; low-selectivity polymer breakdown

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer/polymeric material releases a mixed amino acid inventory that includes alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports alanine formation after hydrolysis of black polymeric HCN products, but not as a clean or selective DAMN-to-alanine transformation. This reaction is therefore explicitly framed as low-specificity hydrolysis of HCN oligomer material yielding alanine among mixed products, correcting the oversimplification noted in prior attempts (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fe-Ni-S-mediated amino acid synthesis including alanine

conditions.catalyst: (Ni,Fe)S-like sulfide surfaces represented by pyrite and greigite
conditions.other: 80-120°C, ~10 days, hydrothermal vent analog conditions; product detected among mixture

reactions.mechanism: Fe/Ni sulfide-mediated carbonyl/thioester-like vent chemistry from CO, cyanide, and thiol feedstocks yields amino acids including alanine in complex product mixtures.
reactions.reasoning: Huber and Wächtershäuser reported hydrothermal synthesis of amino acids including alanine using CO/KCN/CH3SH under Fe/Ni sulfide conditions. This node is intentionally represented as a detected-mixture route rather than an over-specific one-step selective synthesis, addressing prior criticism of overcompression (Huber and Wächtershäuser, 2006).

reactions.name: UV photolysis of interstellar ice analog feedstocks yielding racemic alanine

conditions.pressure: low during irradiation, then 1 atm after deposition
conditions.catalyst: UV photochemistry with mineral trapping after deposition
conditions.other: interstellar-ice analog photolysis followed by delivery/deposition to surface reservoirs

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation of simple ice mixtures generates amino acids in the organic residue; alanine is recovered after warm-up and analysis.
reactions.reasoning: Bernstein et al. and Muñoz Caro et al. showed alanine production from UV photolysis of H2O/CH3OH/NH3/HCN ice analogs. Because the final network must remain terrestrially connected, this is represented as exogenous feed into the surface inventory, with mineral agents included as depositional concentrators in accordance with the schema requirement for surface reactions (Bernstein et al., 2002; Muñoz Caro et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Photocatalytic D-biased alanine enrichment on pyrite surfaces

conditions.other: photocatalytic reductive amination/chiral selection context; reported D-favoring ee 14.5-42.4%

reactions.mechanism: Photocatalytic mineral interactions perturb alanine enantiomeric composition away from exact racemic balance.
reactions.reasoning: Recent work reported natural pyrite photocatalysis causing enantiomeric excesses favoring D-amino acids during alanine-related reductive amination chemistry. This reaction is included not as a route to the target but as a literature-grounded reminder that direct mineral-mediated enantioenrichment for alanine is known and may oppose L-enrichment, which the network must acknowledge rather than ignore (2024 Nature Communications study summarized in the provided review).

reactions.name: Network-level emergence of L-alanine through terrestrial chiral amplification and selection

conditions.catalyst: none singular; emergent selection in mineral interfaces, protocells, and coded-peptide contexts
conditions.other: iterative dissolution-crystallization, peptide ligation selection, magnetite/CISS-informed symmetry breaking elsewhere in the network; not a single validated alanine-specific reaction

reactions.mechanism: Small enantiomeric perturbations generated elsewhere in the prebiotic system are amplified through network-level selection, crystallization, and stereoselective incorporation into emerging biochemical cycles, yielding an L-alanine-enriched pool.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review is explicit that all established abiotic alanine syntheses are racemic and that alanine homochirality likely emerged indirectly through broader terrestrial symmetry-breaking networks rather than a direct single-step synthesis. This final node is therefore necessarily a systems-level connection, grounded in the review's discussion of CISS-driven symmetry breaking, amplification by crystallization/eutectic processes, and stereoselective coded peptide emergence, while transparently acknowledging that no experimentally validated direct prebiotic conversion of DL-alanine to pure L-alanine is known (Ozturk and Sasselov, 2022; Ozturk et al., 2023; Blackmond et al., 2025; Viedma and Ortiz, 2021).

pathways.name: Classic Miller-Strecker route
pathways.description: Reducing-atmosphere spark chemistry generates HCN and acetaldehyde, which proceed through the canonical Strecker sequence to DL-alanine before later biochemical L-selection.

pathways.name: Volcanic H2S Miller-Strecker route
pathways.description: H2S-rich volcanic discharge produces enhanced alanine precursor inventories, converging on the same Strecker hydrolysis sequence.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde to cyanosulfidic acetaldehyde to Strecker alanine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies formaldehyde; surface cyanosulfidic photochemistry produces acetaldehyde, which then enters the Strecker route.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker route
pathways.description: Surface HCN/H2S/UV/phosphate chemistry converts acetaldehyde directly to α-aminopropionitrile at high yield, then hydrolysis gives alanine.

pathways.name: Pyrite-coupled hydrothermal reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate formed from CO2 is aminated reductively by ammonia on FeS/H2S to pyrite redox chemistry to give alanine.

pathways.name: Mixed-valence iron hydrothermal pyruvate route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate undergoes reductive amination in alkaline iron-rich vent sediments under mixed-valence iron control.

pathways.name: Ni-H2 mild catalytic pyruvate route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate feeds a mild prebiotic Ni/H2 catalytic reductive amination stage that directly yields racemic alanine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route from pyruvate
pathways.description: Pyruvate reacts with cyanide, ammonia, and CO2 to form 5-methylhydantoin, which hydrolyzes through N-carbamoyl alanine to alanine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN and related polymeric material, which upon hydrolysis yields alanine among a mixed amino acid inventory.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal Fe-Ni-S complex mixture route
pathways.description: Vent-like Fe/Ni sulfide chemistry from CO, cyanide, and methanethiol produces alanine as one component of a broader hydrothermal amino acid product mixture.
